text: FICAROLO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. of Polesino, 20 m. WSW of Roviego, on the l. bank of the Po, which is here crossed by a drawbridge. Pop. 3,000. It has a port; and carries on a considerable trade in grain, silk, cattle, fish, and wine.
FICHTELBERG, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, presidial and 12 m. N of Kemnath.

FICHTELGEBIRGE, a mountain-plateau in the N of Bavaria, forming a remarkable group in the Hercynian chain. It joins the Böhmerwald to the mountains of Franconia, and these to the Erzgebirge on the E, and the Frankenwald on the WNW. Its culminating points, the Schneeberge, alt. 536 toises or 3,437 ft., and the Ochsenkopf, alt. 532 toises, or 3,402 ft., are nearly under the parallel of 50° N. Several large rivers descend from the E; among these, the Saale flows to the N; the Eger to the E; the Naab to the S; and the Main to the W.

FICHTELNAB, a river of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, rising in the Fichtel-see, a morass between Ochsenkopf and Farmleite, and, after forming a confluence with the Nab, falling into the Danube near Regensburg.
FICULLE, a town of the Pontifical states, in the deleg. and 12 m. NNW of Orvieto, near the r. bank of the Argente. Pop. 1,766. It has some Roman remains.

FIDA, or FEITAN, a prov. of Japan, in the centre of the island of Nifon; bounded on the N by the prov. of Yetsiou, on the E by that of Sinano, on the S by that of Mino, and on the W by the provs. of Yetsizen and Kaga. It is covered with woody mountains, and possesses but little cultivation. It is divided into 4 districts, viz. Ofara, Masita, Araki, and Amano. The capital, Taka-yama or Kao-chan, is situated near the centre of the prov.

FIDALA, or FIDALLAH, a town of Marocco, in the prov. of Shawiya, 42 m. SW of Sali, near the coast, and opposite a small island of the same name. It is of rectangular form, and is enclosed by high walls flanked with towers. It has a fine mosque, and possesses a good roadstead. The surrounding district is fertile; and the trade in its productions, especially in grain, was formerly considerable. The inhabitants, amongst whom are a number of Jews, are now extremely poor.

FIDALGO (PORT), an inlet on the W coast of Russian America, in Prince William sound, extending in a NE direction about 8 leagues, and terminating in N lat. 60° 50', its width being in general upwards of 2 m. Along the shores are extensive plains covered with trees, beyond which rises a chain of lofty mountains clothed in perpetual frost and snow. This spacious inlet having been visited in 1790 by Fidalgo, a Spanish officer, Vancouver, to commemorate his labour, distinguished it by its present name.
FIDARI. See EVENUS.
FIDDEL, a small island in the Elbe, belonging to Hamburg, and 1 m. SSE of that city.
FIDDICH, a river in the co. of Banff, which falls into the Spey 1 m. below Elchies.
FIDDICHOW, a town of Prussian Pomerania, in the circle of Grieffenhagen, on the r. bank of the Oder, 20 m. SW of Stettin. Pop. 2,150.

FIDDINGTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 6 m. WNW of Bridgewater. Area 1,070 acres. Pop. in 1831, 201; in 1841, 220.—Also a township in the p. of Ashchurch, Gloucestershire, 3 m. ESE of Tewkesbury. Pop. with Natton, in 1841, 194.
FIDDOWN, a parish and village in co. Kilkenny, 10 m. WNW of Waterford. Area 11,109 acres. Pop. 4,519.—The v. stands adjacent to the Suir, on the road from Waterford to Clonmel. Pop. 280.
FIDELAIRE (LA), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Eure, 16 m. WSW of Evreux. Pop. 1,551.
FIDEMEN. See FEDMEN.
FIDERIS, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Grisons, 12 m. ENE of Chur, on the l. bank of the Lanquart. Pop. 500.
FIDES, a town of Lower Austria, on the Theya, 6 m. SW of Bohmisch-Wadhofen.
FIDJI. See Fiji.
FIDULCE, an island in the Grecian archipelago, NE of Nauplia, in N lat. 36° 31'.
FIELDBERG, a village of Norway, on a small island in the Hardangerfiord, 51 m. SSE of Bergen.
FIELD-DALLING, a parish of Norfolk, 4 m. ENE of New Walsingham. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. in 1831, 400; in 1841, 403.
FIELDONESS-OEN, a small island of Norway, in N lat. 63° 37', lying off the NE end of Hitteren.

FIDONISI, or the ISLAND OF SERPENTS, a small uninhabited rock in the Caspian. It is a calcareous rock, about 30 yds. high, and not above 600 yds. in its greatest diam. It was anciently called Leuce, and was dedicated to Achilles.
FIEF-SAUVIN (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Montrevault. Pop. 1,769.
FIEGO, or Fijugo, a seaport of Japan, on the S coast of the island of Nifon, with a large harbour, in the bay of Osaca, 24 m. SSW of Osaca, and 48 m. SW of Meaco.
FIEN, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Irak, 5 m. SW of Cashan.
FIENNES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, 3 m. S of Guines, and 45 m. N by E of Boulogne.
FIENVILLERS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Somme, cant. of Bernaville. Pop. 1,193.
FIER, a river of Savoy, which rises at the foot of Mount Chervin, 6 m. SE of Thönes, and falls into the Rhone near Seissel, after a course from E to W of about 45 m.

FIESOLE, a village of Tuscany, in the prov. and 4 m. NE of Florence, on a hill "precipitously steep," overlooking the Val-d'Arno. The hill is cut into a series of narrow terraces, enclosed in a trellis of vines, and faced with loose stone walls. On the very summit of the hill, at an alt. of 1,092 ft. above sealevel, stands a Franciscan convent. The hill of F. itself, clothed with groves of pine, ilex, and cyprus, -its double summit,-the little verdant plain lying between, varied by the broken walls of an ancient Roman amphitheatre, —its scattered village, —its rocks, convents, casinos, hanging-gardens, vineyards, and olive grounds,-form by far the finest mass of scenery in every view near Florence. F. is a place of great antiquity, having been one of the twelve cities of Etruria, and the spot to which Catiline retired on the discovery of his conspiracy. Though a bishop's see, it is at present little more than a heap of ruins, having been in fact dismantled by the Florentines in 1010; but the situation is elevated and healthy, and $ FIF the Florentines have villas here to which they occasionally retire.
FIESSO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. of Polesina, 12 m. SW of Rovigo. Pop. 2,780.

FIFEHEAD, a parish in Dorsetshire, 6 m. WSW of Shaftesbury. Area 1,070 acres. Pop. in 1831, 241; in 1841, 229.—Also a parish in the same co., 9 m. WNW of Blandford-Forum. Area 2,310 acres. Pop. in 1831, 101; in 1841, 83.
FIFENESS, a promontory on the coast of Fife, in N lat. 56° 17′, W long. 2° 36′, 5 m. N by E of the May island, 2 m. E by N of Crail, and SW by W 10 m. from the Bell rock.

FIFESHIRE, a county forming the E portion of the central district of Scotland, being nearly in the middle of the great basin, of which the primitive mountain-ranges of the Lammermuirs on the S, and the Grampians on the N, constitute the boundaries. Its form is peninsular, being enclosed by the German ocean on the E; and by the Friths of Tay and Forth on the N and S; on the W it is bounded by the shires of Perth, Clackmannan, and Kinross, the last of which it almost encloses, except on the W and NW, where it joins Perthshire. The W boundary -the line of which is very irregular-is about 23 m. from its extreme N point on the Tay to the corresponding S point on the Forth. The co. gradually contracts to the E, and finally terminates in the narrow projecting headland of Fifeness. The greatest length from E to W, along the shore of the Forth, is 41 m.; about the centre, in the same direction, from St. Andrews to Loch Leven, it is 231 m. Its breadth across the centre, from Balambreich point on the N, to Leven on the S, is 14 m. The S coast is, for the most part, indented by small rocky bays with corresponding projecting headlands; but along the banks of the Tay, the grounds slope gently towards the beach, and are generally cultivated to the river's edge. Along the NE shore, towards St. Andrews, it presents one large plain, terminating in a flat beach of sand containing a considerable number of broken shells.—According to Sir John Sinclair's General Report of Scotland, the number of cultivated acres in this co., about 25 years ago, was 209,226; of uncultivated, 89,664. Playfair estimates the superficies at 500 sq. m., of which about four-fifths are arable. Macculloch estimates the total area at 300,800 acres. In the late Statistical account the area is stated at 322,560 acres. Physical features.] The general surface partakes more of the gentle, undulating outline of the middle districts of England, than of those bolder and more striking aspects of Nature which characterize the scenery of Caledonia. The Ochils, which skirt its N boundary, and the Lomonds, which run nearly parallel to the Ochils, divide the county into three well-defined districts, which correspond to three equally marked subordinate geognostic formations. These two ranges of hills—which attain their greatest elevation towards the W-are separated by the intervening and finely-wooded valley of Stratheden, in the centre of which the county-town of Cupar is beautifully situated. The ground, on the south of the Lomonds, stretches out in a broad uneven surface towards the Forth; eastwards there rises an elevated table-land, which forms what is characteristically termed 'the Muirs of Fife,' but which gradually merges in the rich and extensive plains, locally designated 'the East Neuk,' comprising an extent of several parishes. Besides the Tay and Forth, which traverse the confines of the co., three rivers of comparatively small dimensions, but of considerable mercantile importance, flow through the district. These are the Eden, which takes its rise near the W extremity of the shire, and after a course of about 18 m., falls into the sea near the bay of St. Andrews; the Leven, which issues from the loch of the same name, and runs along the S escarpment of the Lomonds; and the Orr, which rises in the SW corner of the co., and joins the Leven a few miles to the N of Largo bay, into which they pour their united waters.—The lochs connected with the co. are, Loch- Fitty, Loch-Gelly, Loch-Leven, Loch-Mill, the Black loch, Lindores, and Kilconquhar: all of which are stocked with pike and perch, and some with trout. Geology.] The co. of F., from the one extremity to the other, is exclusively connected with rocks of the medial or carboniferous order. Irregularly mixed up with these, the various members of the trap family are also to be found throughout the length and breadth of the district. The old red sandstone rocks of F. are of comparatively limited extent, and are almost exclusively confined to its N division. Mountain limestone, as it occurs in F., forms a kind of crescent around the out-crop of the coal-field, ranging from the SW extremity of the co. at Broomhall, and passing through the p. of Cleish towards the Lomonds, where it attains an elevation of 1,100 ft. above the level of the sea. It emerges at Randerston on the SE confines of the co Between the bounding line now traced, and the Frith of Forth on the S, this limestone may be considered as occupying much of the intermediate district. From Pettycur to Inverkeithing, the stratified rocks are much intersected and disturbed by those of an igneous origin. The coal-metals of F. are chiefly distinguished by the proportion of bitumen which they yield. At the different coal-fields there are 62 pits open, and upwards of 2,500 men and boys employed. The extent of surface occupied by the coal metals varies from 6 to about 9 m. in breadth from Torry to Pittenweem, the SE point of the basin, is 35 m.; and from Blairadam to Drumcarro, along the line of the N out-crop, is 22 m. There is thus an area of rather more than 200 sq. m. included within the coal-field of Fife. Basalt occupies almost exclusively the S boundary of the shire, along the shores of the Forth. Clinkstone generally forms the cap or highest portion of the Ochil ridge, but by no means uniformly so. The Lomonds are capped with greenstone and amygdaloid.—Alluvium is confined almost to the NW section of the co., and the valley of Stratheden, and at a few places along the banks of the Leven and Orr.—The district intervening between Ferry-Port-on-Craig and St. Andrews furnishes the only example, in the co., of sand-drift, which, although considerable in extent, does not attain in any part of the line an elevation of more than 40 to 50 ft. Peat-moss exists in great abundance, and occupies generally the highest table-land in the district. Agriculture.] The progress of agriculture in F. has been very great since the end of the 18th cent. About four-fifths of the co. is considered as arable land.—The cultivation of oats is more extensive in F. than that of any other sort of grain. The quantity of land annually sown with this kind of grain cannot be computed at less than 30,000 acres. Barley is cultivated to a very considerable extent. Many parts of the co. are well adapted for wheat, and excellent crops of this grain are frequently reared here. Beans and pease are cultivated to the extent of 6,000 acres annually. The quantity of land annually under potatoes cannot be less than 7,000 acres. Few cos. in Scotland at one time cultivated more flax than F.; but the almost universal adoption of cotton-goods has in a great measure abolished the practice, excepting a small patch annually to supply family deficiencies. One great advantage possessed by the Fife agriculturists over those of more inland cos., is, that there is not a farm in the co. 10 m. distant from a seaport. They have, therefore, the important benefit of water-carriage, and are enabled with ease to send their produce to Edinburgh, Glasgow, or London. The size of the farms ranges from 50 to 500 acres. Franchise, &c.] This co. is represented in parliament by one member. The constituency in 1839 was 2,967; in 1848, 2,439. Two sets of royal burghs within this co. also send a member each to parliament. Cupar, St. Andrew's, E. and W. Anstruther, Pittenweem, Kilrenny, and Crail, elect one member; Kirkcaldy, Dysart, Kinghorn, and Burntisland, elect another; Dunfermline and Inverkeithing are conjoined with the Stirling district of burghs in the election of a third.—F. contains 61 parishes, distributed into 4 presbyteries, and which together form the pro- | and town of France, in the dep. of the Lot.-The acces and accessors as a series of the series of the series of the series of the series of the series of the series of the series of the series of the series of the series of the series of the series of the series of the s FIG vincial synod of F.—The number of parochial schools in 1834 was 55, attended by about 4,000 children; of schools not parochial, 223, attended by about 10,000 children.-The annual value of real property in the co. in 1815 was £405,770 sterling. Taking this at 20 years' purchase as the average for the whole, the value of the heritable property in the co. at that time would be, according to this return, £8,115,400. In 1842-3, the annual value of real property was returned at £508,922. In 1811 the land rent was estimated at £335,290; in 1842-3, at £381,572. The pop. of F. appears to be upon the increase, though not so rapidly as in some other parts of Scotland. In 1801 the total pop. was 93,473; in 1811, 101,272; in 1821, 114,550; in 1831, 128,800; in 1841, 140,140. -The number of poor on the roll for the year ending May 5th, 1850, was 4,169; the total expenditure for that year, £25,152. Manufactures.] The principal manufacture in F. has long been that of linen, which, from small beginnings, has gradually increased to its present great importance. Many mills have been erected-and these are still increasing-for the spinning of tow and flax into different qualities of yarn. The cloths woven are of various kinds: sail-cloth, bed-ticking, brown linen, dowlas, duck, checks, shirting, and table-linen. The damask manufacture of Dunfermline is probably unequalled in the world, for the beauty of its design, and the skill with which it is executed. Iron-founding and the making of machinery is carried on in different places. Salt is still manufactured in the co., though not to the extent it formerly was. The tanning of leather is also carried on in two or three localities. Bricks and tiles are made for local use; and earthenware and china manufactured to some extent. Ship-building also forms a part of the trade of the co. History.] The aboriginal inhabitants of F. were Celts; and here, as in other parts of Europe, the names of the more remarkable natural features of the country, as well as of most of the towns, demonstrate the fact. At the period of the Roman invasion, the peninsula between the Forth and the Tay was inhabited by the Horestii. Of the kingdom of the Picts, which seems to have extended throughout the whole of the E coast, and the central portion of Scotland, N of the Roman wall, the co. of F., and the lower portion of Perthshire and Angus, formed the most important portion. In very early times, the Maormors or Earls of Fife were entitled, 1st, to place the king of Scotland on the inaugural stone; 2d, to lead the van of the king's army into battle; and 3d, to enjoy the privilege of a sanctuary to the clan Macduff. In 1411 the university of St. Andrew's was founded by the learned and worthy prelate Henry Wardlaw, bishop of that see. It was in this co. that John Knox first began publicly to deliver his addresses on the antichristian nature of the Papal power.
FIFIELD, a parish of Oxfordshire, 41 m. NNW of Burford. Area 810 acres. Pop. 213.-Also a hamlet in the p. of Bensington, in the same co., 3 m. NE of Wallingford. Area 130 acres. Pop. 21.
FIFIELD-BAVANT, a parish in Wiltshire, 63 m. SW of Wilton. Area 860 acres. Pop. 45.
FIGAMI, an island of Japan, separated by a narrow channel from the E coast of the prov. of Mutsu, in the island of Nifon, on the N side of Senday bay, in N lat. 38° 15'.
FIGANIE'RES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Var, cant. of Callas. Pop. 1,399.
FIGARI (CAPE), a headland of the island of Sardinia, on the NE coast, 6 m. NE of Terra-Nova, in N lat. 41° 2', E long. 9° 40'. It is the Columbarium promontorium of the ancients.
FIGARI (PORTO), a maritime village of the island of Corsica, on the SW coast, in the cant. and 12 m. SE of Sartene. Pop. 500.
FIGASI-SIMA, or Toung-TAO, an island of Japan, in the group of the Go-tu islands, to the NW of the island of Kiusiu.

FIGEAC, an arrondissement, canton, commune, arrond. comprises an area of 159,417 hect., and contains 7 cant., viz.: Cajarc, Saint-Céré, F., Bretenoux, Capelle-Marival, Tronquière, and Livernon. Pop. in 1834, 88,405; in 1846, 92,964.—The cant. comprises 21 com. Pop. in 1831, 34,316; in 1841, 25,525. -The town is 36 m. ENE of Cahors, in a valley on the r. bank of the Selle. Pop. in 1789, 5,538; in 1821, 6,153; in 1831, 6,390; in 1841, 6,171. A college and a school-of-design are its only modern buildings worthy of note, but it has numerous old edifices and the remains of fortifications, and in the environs are 2 remarkable obelisks. It possesses extensive manufactories of linen and cotton fabrics, and several tanneries and dye-works. Fairs for cattle, sheep, and pigs, are held monthly, and general fairs nine times a-year. Lead and coal are found in the vicinity. F. owes its origin to a rich Benedictine abbey, founded by Pepin in 755. It was unsuccessfully besieged by the Calvinists in 1568; but in 1576 it fell into their hands, and was extensively pillaged. Its citadel and other fortifications were destroyed in 1622.
FIGHELDEAN, a parish in Wiltshire, 41 m. N of Amesbury, on the Avon. Area 5,150 acres. Pop. in 1831, 531; in 1841, 510.

FIGHIG, a town of Marocco, in the prov. and 160 m. NE of Tafilet, and 330 m. ENE of Marocco, a station of the caravans from Mecca to Timbuctu. It is said to be a large town; it is noted for its manufacture of fine cloth, and has a considerable trade.
FIGHINE, a town of Tuscany, in the prov. and 53 m. SE of Sienna, and 10 m. E of Radicofani.
FIGHTING ISLAND, an island of Upper Canada, in the Detroit river, 3 m. below Sandwich, comprising an area of about 1,800 acres, of which 300 are fit for cultivation.
FIGI, a maritime town of Japan, in the island of Kiusiu, prov. of Boungo, 15 m. NE of Founaï.

FIGLINE, a town of Tuscany, 23 m. ESE of Florence, on the 1. bank of the Arno. Pop. 4,100. It is well-built, and has one or two handsome streets and squares, and the ruins of a fortress. It formerly possessed extensive manufactories of straw-hats, pottery, and common cutlery; and still carries on a considerable trade in grain, wine, oil, silk, and cattle. The Via Cassia passed over the Arno, by the bridge Degli-Strollé, in this vicinity.

FIGO, or Fei-Heou, a prov. of Japan, in the island of Kiusiu; bounded on the NW by the prov. of Tsikovgo; on the NE by that of Boungo; on the E and SE by the prov. of Fiouga; on the SW by that of Satsouma; and on the W by the bay of Simabara. It is about 60 m. in length, and is bristled in its entire extent by mountains covered with wood and abounding with sulphur. The valleys produce large quantities of corn, legumes, silk, and cotton. This prov. comprises 14 districts, inclusive of the island of Amakousa. The chief town, Koumamoto, is situated near the coast.
FIGO (MONTE), a summit of Portugal, in the Sierra-de-Caldeirao, prov. of Algarve, 15 m. ENE of Faro, in N lat. 37° 10'. Alt. 777 varas, or 2,110 ft.
FIG-TREE BAY, an indentation of the NE coast of Jamaica, in the co. of Surrey, p. of St. George, to the SE of the bay of Anotta, in W long. 76° 45'.

FIGUERA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, comarca and 20 m. ESE of Avis, and 23 m. SW of Portalegre, near the l. bank of the Anhaloura.—Also a town in the prov. of Algarve, 5 m. W of Lagos.—Also an island of Brazil, in the Rio- Doce, at the confluence of the two branches of the Rio Sacuhi.—Also an island of the prov. of São- Paulo, to the SW of the island of Cananea.

FIGUERA, or FIGUERA-DA-Foz, a maritime town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 24 m. W of Coimbra, and 34 m. N of Leiria, on the r. FIJ side of the embouchure of the Mondego. Pop. 6,000. Its harbour is safe and commodious, but difficult of access to vessels of heavy burden. The trade, which is considerable, consists chiefly in salt, oil, wine, dried fruit, oranges, and other articles of local produce.
FIGUERA - DOS - CAVALLEIROS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, 10 m. WNW of Farreira.

FIGUERAS, a judicial partido and town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. of Gerona.—The partido comprises 114 pueblos or communes.—The town is beautifully situated in a fertile plain, between the Muga and Manol, 24 m. NNE of Gerona, and 30 m. S of Perpignan, on the great road from Spain into France. Pop. 8,050. It is ill-built, but its streets are broad, and it has a handsome square surrounded with arcades. It contains a parish-church, 2 convents, and an hospital; and has manufactories of leather and paper. The little commerce which it possesses, consisting chiefly in agricultural produce, it owes to its proximity to France. The citadel, named San Fernando, stands on a height WNW of the town, and is considered one of the strongest fortresses along this line of frontier. It is bomb-proof in all its parts; and contains an arsenal, powder-magazines, barracks for 16,000 men, an hospital, and a church; and is supplied by an aqueduct with water. This fortress was erected about the middle of the 18th cent. In 1808 it was taken by the French; and again in Sept. 1823.
FIGUEREDO-DA-GRANJA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 9 m. SSW of Trancoso, and 17 m. NW of Guarda.

FIGUERO-DOS-VINHOS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, comarca and 24 m. N of Thomar, in a mountainous district, on the l. bank of the Aiso, an affluent of the Zezere, at an alt. of 2,230 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 2,410. The environs afford excellent wine.
FIGUEROLA, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. of Lerida, and partido of Cervera. It has manufactories of linen and of oil.
FIGURAS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, district of Jacobina. It was formerly large and flourishing.

FIGURAS (SERRA), a mountain of Brazil, on the confines of the provs. of Bahia and Goyaz. It forms a continuation of the Mangabeira chain; and gives rise to the Preto or Dourado, and several other affluents of the Rio-Grande.

FIGURIN ISLAND, an inhabited island of the Arctic ocean, in the archipelago of New Siberia, of which it is the most northerly point, to the NW of the island of Fadiefskoy, in N lat. 76°, E long. 140° 30'. It was discovered in 1821 by Lieutenant Anjou.

FIJI, or VITI ISLANDS, a group in the South Pacific, extending between the lat. of 15° 5' and 19° S; and from 177° to 182° E long. It comprises, besides numerous reefs and shoals, 150 islands, 65 of which are inhabited. Their formation is volcanic, and some are very mountainous. The group is a perfect labyrinth of lofty islands and coral reefs, and many disastrous wrecks have occurred in the surrounding seas. They are rich and fertile; yielding sugar-cane, cotton, coffee, cocoa-nut, bread-fruit, oranges, melons, guavas, citrons, and olives; and are visited for beche-de-mer, sandal-wood, and tortoise-shell. Forest-trees abound, and some apparently may be useful in ship-building. On the high grounds Ovalan ferns, parasitical plants, nettles, and wild raspberries are found.—The F. may be subdivided into three groups. 1st, The Weather or E group, comprising Lakemba and the surrounding islands; 2d, The Fiji-levu, or Great-Fiji, sometimes known as Ambow, and its neighbours; 3d, The large North island generally known as Tarkanava.—The inhabitants number about 130,000. They are distinguished above all the Polynesian tribes, excepting, perhaps, the inhabitants of Byron's and Drummond's isles, for treachery and cruelty. They are described as generally above the middle height, the chiefs being tall and well-made; but the lower classes exhibiting the meagreness of frame and meanness of appearance belonging to a state of slavish vassalage among a rude people. The common complexion is a shade between black and copper colour; but both extremes are to be seen. The hair and beard of a F. chief are carefully preserved, and, when dressed, constitute his greatest pride. The office of barber is one of dignity; his hands are tabooed from all other employments, and he is not even allowed to feed himself. The household of a great chief comprises several of these important personages. The hair is frizzled and made to stand erect, extending several inches from the head; a piece of tapa, as fine as tissue paper, is then wound round it as a protection from the dust. When to this is added the tapa round the loins, the ordinary dress of the men is complete. Instead of the tapa, the women wear round the waist a band made from the bark of a tree, the fibres of the lower part being separated. Contrary to the customs of the other islands, tattooing is confined to the females, who regard it as a passport to the other world. They also adorn their persons with flowers. Both sexes disfigure their bodies with black and red pigments, anoint themselves with the offensive cocoa-nut oil, and bore the lobes of the ears, distending the loose flesh by inserting rolls of tapa, pieces of wood, or shells, into the holes. The chiefs wear round the neck a shell, or a necklace of beads, whale's teeth, or human teeth taken from the victims of their cannibal feasts. They spend much time in ornamenting their persons, and "will sit for hours with a small sixpenny looking-glass, admiring themselves with great delight." They are very ingenious, their canoes, houses, arms, clothes, and baskets, being generally of a superior description. They also manufacture earthen vessels with great taste.—The Fijians are divided into a number of tribes; and in each tribe there are five distinct orders of society,-kings, chiefs, warriors, landholders, and slaves,—the last being in a most abject condition. War is the most honourable occupation, and is declared in a formal manner by a herald whose office is held sacred. He is received with great ceremony by the adverse party, to whom he says, on taking leave, "Good bye! It is war!" The usual reply is, "It is well;-return home!" Then hostilities commence, and are conducted with all the treachery and cruelty of savage warfare. The successful party brings home the victims who are to supply the sacrifices to the gods, and furnish the cannibal feasts. Capt. Wilkes insists that "the eating of human flesh is not confined to the cases of sacrifice, but is practised from habit and taste;" and he supports this opinion by some disgusting details. There is a belief, common to all savages, in good and evil spirits; the power of each, and the manner and extent to which the latter are propitiated, depend upon the native character. As the F. are remarkably fierce and cruel, their religious ceremonies are attended with practices even exceeding in horror those of which we have such a thrilling picture in the history of the native Mexicans. The idea, that, after death, they will retain the condition of body and health enjoyed at the time of their decease, leads to the custom of killing aged people, and of self-immolation, to escape from decrepitude and disease. Parents, when they are advanced in years, notify their children that the period has arrived l FIJ when they ought to die. A feast is prepared, the friends bringing presents, and the ceremonies of mourning customary at a funeral are performed. The victim selects the spot for the grave, and the mode of his death, which is generally strangling or burying alive. A parting kiss from all the friends is the signal for placing him in the grave, which, after being covered with sticks and earth, is trodden down. The son visits the spot at night, and lays upon it a piece of ava-root, called the vei-tala, or farewell. Notwithstanding all this, Mr. Hunt, the missionary, assured Capt. Wilkes that the F. were dutiful and kind to their parents, and that this custom was regarded as so great a proof of affection that the children alone were permitted to perform it! A voluntary death is preferred to the prospect of a life afflicted with disease. Persons accidentally maimed, and deformed children, are generally destroyed. Messrs. Hunt and Lythe said they had known only one instance of a natural death during their residence on one of the F. islands. The death of a chief is celebrated by the sacrifice of his wives, his slaves, and even his children. "At the funeral of the late king, Ulivou, which was witnessed by Mr. Cargill, his five wives and a daughter were strangled." Their language is a dialect of the great Polynesian language, and contains a few Malay words. Different dialects, it is said, are in use throughout the group. Lying nearly equidistant from India and America, and contiguous to the shores of New Holland, the F. islands are well-adapted for the purposes of commerce. The Friendly islanders trade with them for canoes, which the F. build in a very superior manner. Articles of barter in request here are muskets, powder whale's teeth, vermilion, calico, and hardware.
FIJIRASAKI, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, prov. of Newa, 84 m. WNW of Nambou.
FIKI, or PY-KY, one of the 21 districts which compose the prov. of Monsasi, in the island of Nifon, Japan.
FIKONE, or YEN-KEN, a town of Japan, capital of the prov. of Oomy, in the island of Nifon, on the E bank of Lake Oitz.
FIL, or FIEL-SOE, a lake of Denmark, in the bail. of Ribe, which discharges itself by a short outlet into the N sea.

FILABRES (SIERRA DE), a mountain-range in Spain, in Andalusia, prov. of Almeria, which detaches itself from the chain of the Alpuxarras at the source of the Almeria, runs 36 m. SE, and unites with the Sierra-de-Gata. Its culminating point is in N lat. 37° 10′, W long. 2° 22′. Alt. 6,270 ft. It contains a fine bed of pure white marble.

FILADELFIA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 14 m. S of Nicastro, in a fine plain. Pop. 3,300. It has 2 churches and several other handsome edifices.—About 3 m. NW of this town is the Fondaco-del-Fico, or the Osteriadi-Cicerone.
FILATHIERA, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Pisa, 5 m. SE of Pontremoli, near the Magra. Pop. 744.
FILATRANO, a town of the Pontifical states, in the del. and 16 m. SW of Ancona.
FILBY, a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. WNW of Cais tor. Area 1,450 acres, of which 160 form a fine sheet of water named Filby-broad. Pop. 553.

FILE-FJELD, a range of mountains in Norway, intersected by the parallel of 61°, and separating N. and S. Bergenhuus, on the W, from Hallingdal and Valdarsdalen on the E. They form a part of the grand chain of the Dofrines; and are connected on the N with the Sogne-field, and on the S with the Hardanger-field.

FILEHNE, or WIELEN, a town of Prussia, in the prov. and 48 m. NW of Posen, regency of Bromberg, circle and 17 m. W of Czarnikau, on an island of the Netz. Pop. 3,480, of whom, in 1837, 1,200 were Jews. It is divided into three parts, distinguished as the Old, New, and Polish; and contains a castle, a Catholic church, a Lutheran chapel, and an hospital. Cloth and lace are manufactured here.
FILET (LE), a village of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Montrevault, 8 m. SSW of St. Florent. Pop. 1,430.
FILETTO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Citra, district and 11 m. SSE of Chieti. Pop. 1,100.
FILEY, a parish and township in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. SE of Scarborough, on the coast. Area of p., 3,090 acres. Pop. 1,590. Area of township, 700 acres. Pop. 1,231.
FILI (SAN), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, district and 8 m. WNW of Cosenza. It has an annual fair.
FILICUDI. See FELICUDI.
FILIGARE, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Florence, 7 m. NW of Ferenzuola. It has a custom-house.

FILINSKOE', a parish and village of Russia in Asia, in the gov. and 135 m. NNE of Tobolsk, on the r. bank of the Irtish. FILIOS, FILBAS, or FILIYAS, a river of Turkey in Asia, in the sanj. of Boli, formed by the union of the Buiuk-su and Kutchuk-su, the first of which descends from the Cheleh-dagh, and the second from the Hermarouz-dagh, and which unite near Boli. Thence the united stream runs N, and, after a total course of about 75 m., falls into the Black sea, a little to the E of a village of the same name. Its principal affluent is the Ova, which it receives on the r. This river is the Billœus of the ancients.
FILIPOVSKAIA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of the Don Cossacks, 6 m. SW of Goughiuskaïa, and 126 m. ENE of Novo-Tcherkask, near the r. bank of the Don.
FILIPOWO, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Augustow, obwod and 36 m. W of Seyny.
FILIPPO D'ARGIRE (SAN), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 13 m. N of Gerace, near the N bank of the Novito. Pop. 1,500.

FILIPPO D'ARGIRO (SAN), a town of Sicily, in the prov. and 36 m. WNW of Catania, district and 11 m. SE of Nicosia. It rises amphitheatrally on an isolated rock, near the r. bank of the Salso, an affluent of the Giaretta, and possesses a fortress and several churches and convents. Pop. in 1831, 7,156. In the vicinity are extensive sulphur works. The cultivation of saffron forms also an important branch of local industry. It is the Agyrium of the ancients, and is noted as the birth-place of Diodorus of Sicily.
FILIPPO (SAN), or BAGNI-DI-SAN-FILIPPO, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. of Sienna, com. and 6 m. N of Badia-San-Salvatore. Pop. 117. It has numerous thermal springs.
FILIPSTADT. See PHILIPSTADT.
FILISUR, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, SE of Alvenem. Pop. 234.
FILKINS, a hamlet in the p. of Broadwell, Oxfordshire, 5 m. SSW of Burford. Area 3,430 acres. Pop. in 1831, 473; in 1841, 556.

FILLAN, a rivulet in the extreme W of Perthshire. It rises on the side of Benloy, on the watershedding mountain-line which forms the boundary with Argyleshire; and, flowing E and N, falls into the W end of Loch Dochart. Its course of 10 m. is through a valley to which the stream gives the name of Strathfillan. FIN

FILLAN (Sr.), a village at the E end of Loch Earn, in the centre of the p. of Comrie, in Perthshire. The village is one of the most pleasant, as to both appearance and situation, in the Highlands of Scotland.
FILLEIGH, a parish in Devon, 33 m. WNW of South Molton. Area 2,260 acres. Pop. 395.
FILLINGES, a village of Savoy, in the prov. of Carouge, 16 m. E of St. Julien, and 12 m. E of Geneva. Pop. 1,360.
FILLINGHAM, a parish in Lincolnshire, 10 m. NNW of Lincoln. Area 3,980 acres. Pop. 312.
FILLOLS, a village of France, in the dep. of the Pyrenees-Orientales, cant. and 4 m. S of Prades. Pop. 250. It has valuable iron-mines.
FILLONGLEY, a parish in Warwickshire, 6 m. NNW of Coventry. Area 4,310 acres. Pop. 1,030.

FILLY, a village of Saxony, in the prov. of Chablais, 7 m. WSW of Thonon, on the S bank of the lake of Geneva. Pop. 265. FILOR, FALOUR, or PHULOUR, a town of the Punjab, 6 m. NW of Ludiana, on the road thence to Umritsir. It is situated on the r. bank of the Sutledge, and is defended by a small fort which is built on a steep bank commanding the river.
FILORINA. See FLORINA.
FILOTI, a small town of Albania, in the pash. and 8 m. W of Joannina.
FILS, or VILS, a river of Würtemberg, which rises 9 m. SE of Kirchheim, at an alt. of 2,040 ft. above sea-level, and falls into the Neckar 4 m. below Esslingen, after a course of 30 m.
FILSOE, a lake of Denmark, in Jutland, in the bail. of Ripen, about a m. from the coast. It is 7 m. in length, and from 2 to 21 m. in breadth.
FILSTROFF, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Bouzonville. Pop. 1,009.
FILTON, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. NNE of Bristol. Area 1,040 acres. Pop. in 1831, 217; in 1841, 276.
FILURINA, a small town of Turkey, 30 m. N of Edessa.
FIMARELLA, a river of Naples, which runs into the gulf of Tarento, in N lat. 39° 36'.
FIMBOROUGH (GREAT and LITTLE), two parishes in Suffolk. Great F. is 3 m. WSW of Stowmarket. Area 1,960 acres. Pop. 467. Little F., 3½ m. SW of Stowmarket, has an area of 220 acres. Pop. 64.
FIMES. See FISMES.
FIMEZI, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, 52 m. WNW of Osaka.
FINA, a river of co. Monaghan, which runs into Lough-Erne 3 m. N of Belturbet.

FINALE, a town of the Sardinian states, situated on the Riviera-di-Ponente, at the end of a beautiful valley, 10 m. NNE of Albenga, and 35 m. SW of Genoa. It consists of two parts, half-a-league distant from each other; the one called F.-Borgo, standing on a hill, the other F.-Marino, situated along the shore. The latter has an insecure harbour, but both divisions are well built, and are defended by three forts, situated on adjacent eminences. The pop. is nearly 8,000. The adjacent country, formerly the marquisate of F., is productive, particularly in olives, oranges, and other fruits. It has repeatedly been the scene of military operations, especially in the outset of Bonaparte's career in April 1796, and at the time of the temporary success of the Austrians in 1800.

FINALE-DI-MODENA, a small town in the duchy of Modena, on the r. bank of the Panaro, between Ferrara and Mirandola, 22 m. NE of Modena. It has manufactories of silk, and some trade in grain. Pop. 7,600. ニーチャン・ナー・カン・コント ディング・フェング・フェング・フェング オーク・フェング・カー・カー・フェング・ファング・ファング・ファング・ファング・ファング・ファング・ファング・ファ
FINANA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 | NNE of Almeria, on the l. bank of the Almanzir. m. N of Almeria, at the foot of Mont Almirez. Pop. 3,000.
FINBY, an island and town of Finland, 28 m. SSE of Abo, in N lat. 60° 7'.
FINCASTLE, a town of Virginia, U. S., cap. of Bottetourt co., situated on the E side of Catabaw ereek, a branch of James river, 116 m. W of Richmond.
FINCHAM, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. ENE of Downham. Area 2,980 acres. Pop. in 1831, 736;
FINDAAS, a parish of Norway, in the prov. and 48 m. S of Bergen, at the entry of the Hardangerfiord. Pop. 3,000.
FINDERN, a chapelry in the p. of Mickleover, 5 m. SSW of Derby. Pop. in 1831, 410; in 1841, 416.

FINDHORN, a river of the cos. of Inverness, Nairn, and Moray. It rises in the Monadleadh hills, between Strathdearn and Stratherrick, in Inverness-shire; and flows in a NE course, through part of Inverness, Nairn, and Moray shires, to a loch, or arm of the sea, called Findhorn harbour, in the Moray frith, at a distance of 60 m. in direct extent from its source, increased, by its windings, to 30 m. more. A low and level district surrounds its estuary; and, during the memorable floods of August 1829, such was the rapid rise of the stream, that the whole plain, to the N and W of Forres, became one sea of waters, so that a large boat in full sail swept along the fields to within a few yards of that burgh! It is crossed only by three bridges,—one at Forres, a second at Dulsie, and a third on the military road from Inverness to Aviemore. There is excellent fishing of salmon in the F. The average produce of the fishing for 12 years, from 1813 to 1824, was 156 barrels of salmon, and 77 barrels of grilses. The F. is navigable for boats no farther than the tide flows.

FINDHORN, or FINDHERN, a small sea-port town, in the p. of Kinloss, Morayshire, situated at the mouth of the Findhorn, on a point of land which is rendered peninsular by the harbour of F. on the W, and the bay of Burgh-head on the E. It is distant 5 m. N of Forres, to which, as well as, in some measure, to Elgin, it is considered as a sea-port. It was long celebrated for curing and drying haddocks. The v. has changed its site more than once. It formerly stood 1 m. to the NW of the present site, but was swallowed up by an inundation of the sea and river, in 1701, and the place where it then stood is now the bottom of the sea. The mouth of the river F. itself was formerly 2 m. to the W of its present situation.
FINDOE, an island off the coast of Norway, in the Bukke-flord, 15 m. ENE of Stavanger, in N lat. 59° 10′.
FINEA, a village in the p. of Favoran, co. Westmeath. Pop. 260.
FINECHIOLARO, a small island in the Mediterrean, near the NE coast of Corsica, 7 m. N of Bastia.
FINEDON, a parish of Northamptonshire, 3 m. NNE of Wellinborough. Area 3,650 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,293; in 1841, 1,378.
FINES, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 36 m. FIN Pop. 1,000.
FINESHADE, a parish in Northamptonshire, 8 m. NW of Oundle. Area 840 acres. Pop. in 1831, 68; in 1841, 55.
FINESTRAT, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 24 m. NE of Alicant, on the river Torres, 3 m. above its mouth. Pop. 2,720.
FINGAMI, a town of Japan, on the island of Nifon, 45 m. N of Meaco.
FINGEST, a parish of Bucks, 53 m. WNW of Great Marlow. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. in 1831, 340; in 1841, 379.
FINGHALL, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. ENE of Middleham. Area 4,460. Pop. in 1831, 127; in 1841, 458.
FINGLASS, a parish and village in co. Dublin. Area of p., 9,024 acres. Pop. 2,187.—The v. is 3 m. N by W of Dublin. Pop. 678.-Also a p. in King's co., 23 m. ENE of Moneygall. Pop. 312.
FINGRINGHOE, a parish in Essex, 4 m. SSE of Colchester. Area 2,490 acres. Pop. in 1831, 542; in 1841, 581.
FINHAN, a town of France, in the dep. of the Tarn-et-Garonne, 10 m. SW of Montauban. Pop. 1,646.
FINIMARBU, a village of Bambarra, in Africa, 96 m. WNW of Sego.
FININGHAM, a parish in Suffolk, 6 m. SW of Eye. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. in 1831, 497; in 1841, 480.
FINISH, an island at the entrance of Kilkerran bay, co. Galway, a ½ m. E of Minish. It is about 1 m. in length.—Also an islet of co. Clare, in the estuary of the Fergus.

FINISTE'RE, a department of France, so named from its forming the NW extremity of the kingdom. It is part of the ancient prov. of Bretagne. It is bounded on the N by the English channel; on the E by the deps. of Cotes-du-Nord and Morbihan; and on the S and W by the Atlantic ocean; and lies between the parallels of 47° 40′ and 48° 40′ N. Its area is 666,622 hectares. None of its rivers are large. The chief are the Aulne, or Aon, which falls into the bay of Brest; the Odet, flowing S by Quimper; and the Laita, which enters from the dep. of Morbihan and passes by Quimperlé. Two granitic chains of hills intersect the dep. from E to W, one terminating near Brest, the other at Crozon.—A great portion of the surface is hilly and rocky, and the climate is wet and changeable. The soil is not fertile, except in particular situations, and there is much waste land. About 259,800 hect. are rich arable land; 268,573 hect. are in heaths and wastes. Rent varies from 5s. to 30s. an acre. The principal productions are grain, flax, hemp, and fruits. The vine is not reared. Broom is cultivated as an article of fuel. The pasturage is good; and horses and cattle are reared in great numbers. In 1830 the stock of horned cattle was 180,500 head; of goats, 70,000; of sheep, 47,300. Bees are largely reared. Iron, lead, coal, and slate, are wrought. The lead-mines of Pouillaouen and Huelgoet are the most extensive in France.—The manufactures are paper, coarse linen, woollen, leather, &c.; and on the coast are abundant fisheries. The coast, especially on the W, is much indented; and it affords numerous good harbours, whose entire tonnage in 1841 amounted to 1,538 vessels = 16,999 tons, and 3 steam-vessels = 107 tons. Off the shore are several small islands belonging to the dep.—On the N is Bas in the English channel. In the Atlantic, about 13 m. from the W coast, lies the Isle of Ouessant or Ushant, the ancient Uxantis; and SE from Ushant are Bannée, Molene, Quemenés, and Binniguel. The Isle of Sein lies off the bay of Audierne. The Glenans are an uninhabited group 20 m. due south from Quimper.-The dep. of F. is divided into the 5 arrondissements of Brest, Quimper, Morlais, Châteaulin, and Quimperlé; which are subdivided into 41 cantons and 282 com. The chief towns are Quimper, Brest, and Morlais.- Pop. in 1836, 546,955; in 1841, 576,068; in 1846, 612,151; of whom 244,491 were collected in towns of upwards of 3,000 souls each.
FINISTERRE, a small town of Spain, in the prov. and 64 m. WSW of La Corogne, near the cape of the same name. It is situated on a cove, and inhabited chiefly by fishers. Pop. 1,000.

FINISTERRE (CAPE), the NW point of Spain, on the coast of Galicia, so called from its having been accounted, before the discovery of America, the W extremity of the globe. It is in N lat. 42° 54', W long. 9° 20' [Raper]; N lat. 42° 56' 30", W long. 9° 13' 22" [Laurie's chart]. There is a bight between it, and the Navé of Finisterre, a mountain 5% m. SSW of Cape Toriana. It is the Artabrum or Nerium promontorium of the Romans.
FINKENVERDER, an island in the Elbe, between Ottensee and Nieusteden, belonging half to Hanover, and half to Hamburgh.

FINLAND, a government or administrative division of Russia, lying between the parallels of 60° and 70° N, and between 21° and 32° E long. On the N it is bounded by Lapland; on the E by the Russian govs. of Archangel and Olonetz; on the S by the gov. of Petersburg and the gulf of Finland; and on the W by Sweden and the gulf of Bothnia. Besides the former Swedish province of the same name, it comprehends all the gov. of Viburg, or the ancient Russian Finland. It stretches to the highest north; and comprehends a territorial surface of 144,000 sq. m., with a pop. of 1,400,000, which is said to be rapidly increasing. On the N and W some branches of the Scandinavian mountains enter this gov.; on the E it is covered with sand-hills and rocks, and intersected by marshes, seas, lakes, and rivers, which diffuse cold and unwholesome mists. The most central lake in the country—the Payana, or 'the Peaceable'—is about 72 m. in length, and 13 m. in breadth. The lake of Saima on the E, which is crowded with islands, is still larger; and discharges its superfluent waters in a series of six cataracts to the Tornea, which carries its waters into the Ladoga lake. The coasts of the Bothnian and Finland gulfs are thickly strewn with granite and limestone rocks, and in some places present a labyrinthine archipelago of little islands. The chief rivers are the Ulea, which is navigated by trading vessels, though its stream is so rapid that ships run down the river at the rate of 18 m. in the hour; the Cano, which passes Biorneborg; the Aurajoki at Abo, about 100 yds. broad, and remarkable for its muddy waters; the Kymen, which flows into the middle of the gulf of Finland; and the Tornea, which discharges itself at the N extremity of the gulf of Bothnia, and is the present boundary between Sweden and Russia. — The mountains of Finland often contain a mixture of felspar and mica. Lead ore is found in various parts, and a ferruginous earth from which iron is extracted. There are, in many places, extensive forests; one particularly, to the N of Abo, is about 80 m. in length. Great devastations are occasioned in these forests by the tempests of winter, which seem to find access to the very centre of the wood, by descending in the manner of a tornado, which tears up by the roots, or breaks in the middle of the trunk, or bends to the earth, the largest pines. Frequently also ravages the point of land which forms the N extremity of by the carelessness of the peasants smoking their pipes and making fires in the woods.—The climate and seasons of F. vary considerably in different parts of the country, according to their latitude and situation. At Uleaborg, the most northerly town, winter commences in October and continues to the end of April; spring is limited to the month of May; summer begins in June, and lasts three months; the autumn, like the spring, is confined to one month, and both commences and terminates in September. Even in summer the night-frosts are sharp, particularly about the middle of August. From the beginning of summer, however, the progress of vegetation, as in most other northern latitudes, is remarkably rapid; and there have been instances of grain being sown and reaped in the neighbourhood of Uleaborg, in the space of six weeks. In the S parts of the country, near the gulf of Finland, the heat in summer is equal to what is generally experienced in Portugal; and the long continuance of the sun above the horizon increases the temp. above that of more southern countries. At Uleaborg, the middle temperature of the four seasons, according to a mean proportion of 12 years, was found to be in winter 10 degrees below zero; in spring and autumn, 6 degrees above the freezing-point; and in summer, 15°. Sir John Carr remarks, that the summer burst upon him in this country with fiery fury on the 11th of July, with no earlier precursor than grass and green leaves. On a sudden, the flies, which experience a longer date of existence in the north than in the milder regions of Europe, awake from their torpor with the arrival of the hot weather, and annoy the traveller beyond imagination.—The surface of the country, besides what is occupied by large lakes and forests, is frequently covered to a great extent with marshes and with masses of stone; but not more so than other parts of Sweden, and, excepting E. Gothland, is not less fertile and populous. The soil is generally of a sandy nature, or a strong clay; it is only in the valleys, or along the banks of rivulets, that earthy mould is to be seen. Agriculture, rearing of cattle, and fishing, are the principal occupations of the inhabitants. The harvest, consisting chiefly of barley and rye, sometimes yields an exportable surplus. In the interior of the country there is good timber for marine purposes. Granite is the principal mineral in this district. Iron was once wrought in F. Proper, but the Finlanders now import that metal from Sweden. A great quantity of nitre is made. The coasts present good harbours; but they cannot be extensively used, as the long winter detains ships six months in harbour. In winter, transport by sledges affords an easy and rapid communication. The following statement of the returns of the trade of F. for the year 1848, has been compiled from official documents. The returns are in silver rubles of the value of 3s. 2d. nearly. EXPORTS. Total, Showing an excess of 476,816 rubles in the imports over the exports, which however was in part covered by the freight earned by the merchant-shipping. The above statements indicate a falling off as compared are committed in them by conflagrations, occasioned | with the year 1847 in the export trade to Sweden and Norway. To those two countries the leading articles of export and their value were: Fish, 23,569 rubles; hides, 8,616; beef, 24,578; grain, 15,083; tallow, 30,809; tar, 11,483; firewood, 11,483.—The imports were: Books, 16,200 rubles; fish, 25,228; iron and steel, 250,870; sugar, 66,418; tobacco, 4,677; dye woods and stuffs, 23,990.—The exports to other countries were also less than in 1847. The principal articles were: potash, 16,427 rubles; pitch, 15,871; hemp and linseed, 16,733; butter, 183,409; tar, 324,717; wood and timber, 921,360. In the imports were: medicines, 20,914; cotton, 138,318; arrack, rum, brandy, 169,794; coffee, 597,708; fruits, 106,042; dye stuffs, 134,550; yarn, 349,933; iron and steel, 89,709; salt, 531,665; sugar, 544,485; wine, 140,528; manufactures wove, 666,145. This gov. is divided into 8 laens or administrative subdivisions, which are further parcelled out into fögderi or harads. The laens, with their area, and pop. in 1835, are: Pop. Area. Uleaborg, . The principal towns are Helsingfors and Abo. The Fins.] The Fins appear to have been the original inhabitants of Sweden, and to have proceeded from their settlements around the White sea, from a country formerly styled Permia or Biarmia, and still exhibiting traces of the ancient prosperity of its inhabitants. They are short in stature, with flat faces, dark grey eyes, a thin beard, tawny hair, and a sallow complexion; but those who inhabit the more S and W districts, while they retain the national features, have a superior appearance, and are scarcely to be distinguished in their manners from the Swedes. In Russian Finland, however, they have a slowness of motion, and almost stupidity of look, which form a striking contrast with the lively aspect, alert movements, and generally cheerful humour of the Russians. More hardy than the Muscovites, they are not so warmly clothed, and seldom wear the sheep skin; but have a coat of coarse woollen stuff, made without regard to shape, and tied round the body with a band, a pair of coarse linen drawers, or loose pantaloons, straw shoes, and pieces of woollen cloth, or ropes of straw, wrapped round their legs. It is chiefly in the N parts of F., or rather in Lapland, that the ancient Finnish language is heard. Along the gulfs of F. and Bothnia the Swedish language prevails in the towns; and the peasantry speak a mixed dialect of both tongues. The F. were subjugated by Eric IX. of Sweden, and converted to the Christian faith in 1156; and from that period, literature, especially poetry, began to be more generally cultivated among them. The verse which they employ is that called Runic, in which the lines consist of eight syllables, a long and a short in succession; but, instead of terminating in rhymes, they begin with alliteration, having at least two or more words which agree in the same letter or sound, as in the following specimen: Nuco nuco pico linto Wessi wessi wester eki. These Runic verses are rarely committed to the press, or even to writing, but are chiefly transmitted by oral communication; and none are found of an earlier date than the era of the Reformation. In the more inland districts, the peasantry are much addicted to this species of composition; and many of them, unaided by education, are capable of producing verses on ordinary subjects, sometimes of considerable merit, and in a great measure extempore. The recitation or composition of such songs, sometimes accompanied with the harp, forms one of the most frequent amusements of the country people at fairs and private meetings. On these occasions, the reciter or poet stands in the midst of a circle of auditors, and having sung or delivered one line, a coadjutor, taking up the last word, or the last but one, finishes the line along with him, and then repeats it alone; which gives the speaker time to recollect or compose the succeeding line, which he then sings, and his assistant repeats in like manner, until the poem is ended. The F. language abounds in proverbs, bearing much resemblance in their form to those of Solomon, and generally expressive of sound sense and acute remark. They are mostly in Runic or alliterative verse, and divided into two hemistichs, the latter of which contains an illustration of the former. Their native musical instrument, the harpu, consists of five strings or chords of metal, each of which sounds a distinct note: and to the compass of these five notes, their music is confined. Their dancing consists in jumping, without any variety of step or motion, except alternately laying the arms over each other. The whole exercise is performed with sufficient vigour, but with entire gravity of countenance. The houses in F., and frequently even the churches and other public edifices, are constructed of wood, generally painted red; but they are nevertheless sufficiently warm, sometimes too much so for the feelings of those not accustomed to a close atmosphere. The habitations of the peasants are well built, and afford complete protection from the severity of the winter cold; and, notwithstanding the long duration of that season, and the seeming sterility of the soil, the people are in many respects better provided than the same class in more southern regions. They can generally set before the traveller at least fresh and curdled milk, salt herrings, or a little salt meat; and they are rich in all that they consider as constituting the enjoyments of life. The women, above their other garments, wear a large linen shift, which gives them the appearance of being in an undress. In the winter season the men wear a kind of short coat made of calf-skin, or a woollen surtout, fastened round the middle with a girdle; and pull over their boots coarse woollen stockings, both for warmth and for safer walking on the ice. Most of the peasants have a small house for the purpose of taking the warm bath, which is done in the following manner: - A number of stones in the innermost part of the chamber are heated by fire till they become red; and water being thrown upon them in this state, the bathers are involved in a cloud of thick vapour, in an atmosphere of 70 or 75 degrees of Celsius. In this heated and oppressive air, they will remain naked for the space of half-an-hour, or even a whole hour, rubbing their bodies with bunches of twigs; and will then frequently go out without any covering to the open air, or roll themselves in the snow, when the degree of cold is 20° or 30° below zero; thus house and abroad. Within doors they are engaged in making nets, constructing cart-wheels, forming faggots for fuel, or thrashing corn; out of doors, they cut down timber, and at this season drag over the ice or snow trees they could scarcely move in summer. Fishing and hunting are their principal avocations. They kill squirrels and other small furred animals with a blunt wooden arrow, shot from a cross-bow, that they may not injure the skin. The bow thus used is of very ancient construction, extremely heavy, and requiring great strength to bend it, even with the assistance of a thong. They are dexterous both in the use of this bow, and of the fowling-piece. Winter is their principal season of traffic; all the great fairs are held in Finland and Sweden in that season, in consequence of the facility of earrying goods over the ice, and travelling in sledges on the snow. The peasants on these occasions frequently undertake journeys of 300 or 400 m., carrying along with them whatever articles they have for sale. Their sledges are narrow, containing only one person, and drawn by a single horse; and the roads are deep ruts formed by the successive passage of these vehicles, and admitting none of a larger size than what are generally used in the country. The Finnish race, estimated to be about 2,400,000 in number, is scattered over a wide extent of territory, extending from the Scandinavian peninsula, along the N coast of Europe, far into the N of Asia, and from thence to the Volga and the Caspian sea. Tacitus was acquainted with a race called Fenni, whose favourite residence was the woods and morasses of the north. They styled themselves 'the inhabitants of the morasses;' and were principally occupied in hunting and fishing. It is worthy of remark, that the scattered Finnish tribes all retain the national physiognomy, character, language, and manners, to such a degree as to be easily recognised. They have no independent history. In their simple wandering life they were the ready prey of the Norwegians, Swedes, and Russians. The Norwegians first subdued Finmark. Their expeditions against the Permians, a tribe of F. on the White sea, continued till the princes of Novogorod had become masters of Permia and the trade thither, and the Norwegians found occupation in repelling the incursions of the Mongols. The Russians next began to extend their authority in the territory of the F., Karelia, and all Permia fell under their power; and in the 14th cent. the Cross was erected on the shores of the White sea by Bishop Stephen, and the temple of Jomala destroyed. All Lapmark, and the F. on the Volga and in Siberia, were now reduced by the Russians, who also drove back the Norwegians, who attempted to maintain their earlier encroachments in Lapmark. Last of all, the Swedes attacked the F. residing on their borders. In the middle of the 12th cent., St. Eric converted the inhabitants of the present Finland to Christianity, and a century later the Swedes subdued Tavastland, and also those parts of Karalia and Lapland not then belonging to Russia. The subjugation of the F. tribes in the N was now complete; twelve of their tribes, wholly or in part, became subjects of Russia, embracing Laplanders, F., Esthonians, Livonians, Tschuvashes, Mordvines, and a few Tartars. The Tscheremisses and Tschuvashes resemble the Tartars; the Mordvines, the Russians, and the Vogules, resemble the Calmucks. The F. are principally Christians either of the Lutheran or the Greek church. But among the Tscheremisses, Mordvines, Votiacks, and Vogules, there are still some families professing Shamanism. -Coxe's Travels in Russia, &c.-Acerbi's Travels in Sweden, &c.- Wraxal's Tour round the Baltic .- Swinton's Travels into Norway. -Clarke's Travels.-Dillon's Journal.

FINLAND (GULF OF), a large arm of the Baltic, stretching between the parallels of 58° 56' and 60° 37' N, and between 21° 45' and 29° 57' E long. Its coasts are now wholly Russian, having Finland on the N; the Russian gov. of Petersburg on the E.; and that of Esthonia or Revel on the S. Its length from Hangohead, in N lat. 59° 46′ 30″, E long. 22° 59′, is 73 leagues; but measured from the meridian of 22° to its extreme point, along the parallel of 60°, it is 240 m. in length. Its breadth varies from 4 to 20 leagues. Its depth varies from 50 to 60 fath. in mid channel; in the bay of Cronstadt, its E extremity, it is only 2 fath. Its currents vary with the wind. It contains numerous small islands, the principal of which are Dago at its S entrance, Hoogland, on the meridian of 26° 57' E, Sommar N ½ E 21 m. from Hoogland, Kotlin isle on which Cronstadt stands, and the Great Tyters. The principal ports are Hapsel on the isle of Dago and Port Baltic with Revel, which close in November or December, and open in February or March; and Naroa, Petersburg, Vyborg, and Fredericksgam, which close in October or November, and open in April. The principal rivers which discharge themselves into this gulf are the Luga, the Narva, and the Neva.

FINMARK, or FINMARKEN, a bailiwick or administrative prov. of Norway, forming the N extremity of the great Scandinavian peninsula, and sometimes called Norwegian Lapland. On the N and NE it is bounded by the Arctic ocean; on the E by Russia; on the S by the prov. of Nordland; and on the W it has the Arctic ocean. It lies between the parallels of 68° 18' and 71° 10' N. Its length from SW to NE exceeds 300 m.; its greatest breadth is about 180 m. Its area amounts to 626 Swedish sq. m. Its pop. in 1801 was estimated at 26,895; in 1825 at 30,528; in 1835, at 37,504; in 1845, at 36,854. The chief rivers are the Alten, flowing through the centre with Russia; and the Malms, which intersects the SW districts. The shore is broken by several of those long arms of the sea called fiords, among which the most remarkable are the Alten-fiord, the Porsangers-fiord, and the Tana-fiord. The coasts are deeply indented; and many mountainous and rocky islands lie at a short distance from them. The chief of these islands are Senjan, Hvalöen, Ringvadsöe, Hasväg or Soroe, Mageroe, and Vardehuus.—Vegetation in this prov. is scanty; the birch is almost the only tree; grass grows in the recesses amongst the mountains, but on approaching the North cape, only rein-deer moss, and a few Alpine plants are found. F. is administratively annexed to the stift of Drontheim, and is subdivided into the 3 fögderi of Hammerfest, Tromsöe, and Vardöe.
FINMERE, a parish in Oxfordshire, 8 m. NNE of Bicester. Area 1,650 acres. Pop. in 1831, 373; in 1841, 387.
FINN, a river of Ireland, which rises in a small lake of the same name, in co. Donegal, and runs into Lough Foyle, near Strabane, after an E course of about 24 m.
FINNAN, or FINDON, a fishing village in the p. of Banchory-Davenick, 6 m. S of Aberdeen.
FINNAN, a river of Inverness-shire, which falls into the E extremity of Loch-Shiel.
FINNERYDIA, a small town of Sweden, in W. Gothland, 34 m. SW of Orebro, and 86 m. NE of Uddevalla.
FINNHAR, a small island on the W side of the gulf of Bothnia, in N lat. 60° 58'.
FINNINGHAM, a parish of Suffolk, 6 m. SSW of Eye. Area 1,560 acres. Pop. in 1831, 497; in 1841, 480.
FINNINGLEY, a parish of Yorkshire and Nottinghamshire, 4 m. NE of Bawtry. Area 5,970 acres. Pop. 1,209.
